questions.text: I tend to absorb emotional information without it disrupting my internal stability.
I find that decorating my space exactly to my taste improves my daily mood and output.
I pick up on the way someone's breathing changes when they are uncomfortable.
I believe that the moral weight of suffering is the consideration that should humble every other claim on my attention and resources.
I experience the buildup to an important event as a nearly unbearable crescendo of feeling.
I hold myself accountable when I fail to respond to suffering that I was aware of.
I think that the discipline of remaining emotionally composed under pressure is one of the most practical skills a person can have.
I know a project is done when it feels complete, not when it meets predefined criteria.
I believe that pouring myself out for others is the most fulfilling way to spend my energy.
I hold the view that my morning routine should be designed entirely around my own optimal performance.
I often yield to the group's preferred approach, even if mine differs.
I find that I naturally read the room before deciding how to contribute to a conversation.
I prefer working with people who say what they think rather than hinting.
I find that emotional distance from the details of a case allows me to make recommendations that are more consistent and less biased.
I experience the first day at a new place as a sensory and emotional deluge.
I am usually the one who interviews and selects new team members.
I experience the anticipation before a big moment as a mild heightening of attention, not a surge of adrenaline.
I routinely set personal records and benchmarks to push myself further.
I feel uneasy when I don't have a clear plan for the week ahead.
I detect when someone positions themselves physically between two people to mediate or interrupt a connection.
I believe that the discipline of maintaining a level-headed perspective during crises is what transforms good intentions into effective outcomes.
I notice that I frequently steer conversations toward action.
I rarely pick up on the social choreography that determines who walks next to whom.
I read contracts and agreements thoroughly before signing them.
I offer feedback only when asked or when the group has a feedback culture in place.
I research until I feel I have enough rather than imposing time limits on exploration.
I hold the view that true success is measured by how much you improve others' lives.
I like to personally onboard new members and set their expectations.
I notice that my voice changes — gets higher, tighter, or shakier — when I'm emotionally activated.
I do not tend to analyze social interactions after they happen.
I let daily activities be guided by what excites me rather than by a long-term vision.
I believe that if my plate is full and someone else's is empty, something on my plate belongs to them.
I keep a log of what I accomplish each day to maintain awareness of my productivity.
My emotional baseline is so stable that people often describe me as having a calming presence.
I feel that the moral weight of another's pain creates an imperative that is difficult to override with any competing consideration.
I feel that ambiguity is an invitation for the group to explore possibilities together.
